intro: DiveSZN will always guide you to choose your gear the best way — independently, honestly and with your diving in mind — so the only thing you're thinking about at twenty metres is what just swam out of the blue. In every guide below we rank the ten models divers and reviewers trust most, tell you what actually matters before you pay, and point you to the three cheapest places we found to buy — because the same regulator shouldn't cost you two hundred dollars more for the same box.

channels.name: DALLMYD
channels.focus: Scuba treasure hunting, river/lake recovery dives

channels.name: Man + River
channels.focus: Underwater treasure hunting and freshwater diving

channels.name: Jiggin' With Jordan
channels.focus: River treasure diving and lost-item recovery

channels.name: Jonathan Bird's Blue World
channels.focus: Emmy-winning underwater science and dive adventures

channels.name: Aquachigger
channels.focus: River diving, relics and underwater exploration

channels.name: DIVE TALK
channels.focus: Cave divers react: incidents, safety, dive culture

channels.name: Everything Scuba (Scuba Diver Magazine)
channels.focus: Dive news, gear reviews and travel with Mark Newman

channels.name: Simply Scuba
channels.focus: Dive gear reviews, unboxings and scuba tips (UK)

channels.name: Divers Ready
channels.focus: Dive skills, training and no-nonsense gear advice

channels.name: PADI
channels.focus: Official training org: skills, travel, conservation

channels.name: Dive SAGA
channels.focus: Tech, cave and wreck diving adventures plus gear

channels.name: Alec Peirce Scuba
channels.focus: Scuba Tech Tips, gear know-how and vintage diving

channels.name: Koox Diving
channels.focus: Cenote and cave diving in Mexico, dive training

channels.name: Lake Hickory Scuba
channels.focus: Dive training, gear service tips from a NC dive shop

channels.name: Girls that Scuba
channels.focus: World's biggest women's dive community, travel tips

channels.name: 50ft Below
channels.focus: Honest scuba gear reviews and buying advice

channels.name: Azul Unlimited
channels.focus: Scuba education, dive lifestyle and travel vlogs

channels.name: DAN - Divers Alert Network
channels.focus: Dive safety, medicine and incident prevention

channels.name: Deep Dive Dubai
channels.focus: World's deepest pool: dive experiences and training

channels.name: Scuba Diving Magazine
channels.focus: Gear tests, training and dive travel from PADI media

items.name: Atomic Aquatics Venom Frameless
items.blurb: A premium frameless mask built around ultra-clear glass that removes the green tint of standard lenses, so colours at depth stay true — a real advantage on coral and for photographers.
items.review: A premium frameless mask built around ultra-clear glass that removes the green tint of standard lenses, so colours at depth stay true — a real advantage on coral and for photographers. The dual-silicone skirt pairs an ultra-soft sealing edge with a firmer supporting base, holding its seal over smile lines and slim cheekbones alike. It folds flat into a BCD pocket, and the wide single lens keeps the view open and unobstructed.

items.name: Scubapro Synergy 2 Trufit
items.blurb: The skirt blends two silicone densities — supple where it touches your face, firmer where the mask needs structure — so it seals on a far wider range of faces than single-density masks.
items.review: The skirt blends two silicone densities — supple where it touches your face, firmer where the mask needs structure — so it seals on a far wider range of faces than single-density masks. Divers who struggle with persistent leaks usually end their search here. Twin tempered lenses sit close to the eyes for low internal volume that clears with a short exhale, and the push-button buckles adjust easily in gloves.

items.name: Cressi Big Eyes Evolution
items.blurb: Inverted teardrop lenses angle downward, adding real visibility toward your chest — gauges, inflator, camera — without tilting your head.
items.review: Inverted teardrop lenses angle downward, adding real visibility toward your chest — gauges, inflator, camera — without tilting your head. The crystal silicone skirt keeps the view bright and airy, welcome for anyone who finds black-skirt masks closed-in. Decades on the market and still current: dependable seal, sensible price, and lens geometry newer designs haven't beaten.

items.name: Mares X-Vision
items.blurb: Oversized twin lenses in a slim frame give one of the widest fields of view of any two-lens mask.
items.review: Oversized twin lenses in a slim frame give one of the widest fields of view of any two-lens mask. Ribbed skirt sides stiffen the seal against current without pressing on the face, and the frame accepts factory optical lenses from −1.0 to −7.0, so short-sighted divers can fit prescription glass at the dive shop. Best fit on medium to wide faces.

items.name: Aqua Lung Reveal X2
items.blurb: Buckles mounted on rotating cardanic joints let the strap find the natural angle of the back of your head, spreading pressure evenly — no hot spots after an hour underwater.
items.review: Buckles mounted on rotating cardanic joints let the strap find the natural angle of the back of your head, spreading pressure evenly — no hot spots after an hour underwater. Volume stays low for easy clearing, and the double-feathered skirt edge favours narrow-to-medium faces. A quietly excellent all-rounder that shops reach for when other masks won't fit a customer.

items.name: TUSA Freedom Ceos M-212
items.blurb: TUSA's Freedom skirt varies its thickness and surface texture along the seal line — smooth where it needs grip, dimpled where the face flexes — and the result is a mask that stays sealed through smiling, talking into a regulator and equalising.
items.review: TUSA's Freedom skirt varies its thickness and surface texture along the seal line — smooth where it needs grip, dimpled where the face flexes — and the result is a mask that stays sealed through smiling, talking into a regulator and equalising. Low-profile twin lenses accept corrective lenses, and the colour range is the widest in any dive shop.

items.name: Hollis M1 Frameless
items.blurb: A single-lens frameless with genuinely low internal volume: it clears with a puff and mask-equalises almost automatically, which is why it became a standard among technical divers.
items.review: A single-lens frameless with genuinely low internal volume: it clears with a puff and mask-equalises almost automatically, which is why it became a standard among technical divers. The wide tempered lens keeps sightlines open in poor visibility, the matte black skirt cuts glare and seals under a hood, and folded flat it lives in a thigh pocket as a backup until it becomes your primary.

items.name: Scubapro Frameless
items.blurb: The design that proved a mask needs no frame: the skirt bonds directly to one large tempered lens, giving a wide view, low volume and a mask that packs completely flat.
items.review: The design that proved a mask needs no frame: the skirt bonds directly to one large tempered lens, giving a wide view, low volume and a mask that packs completely flat. There is nothing to loosen, crack or corrode on a boat deck. The generous lens suits medium and larger faces, and the value-per-dive is among the best in diving.

items.name: Atomic Aquatics SubFrame
items.blurb: Atomic solved the one weakness of frameless masks — the slow distortion of an unsupported skirt — by moulding a rigid skeleton inside the silicone, invisible from outside and immune to flex.
items.review: Atomic solved the one weakness of frameless masks — the slow distortion of an unsupported skirt — by moulding a rigid skeleton inside the silicone, invisible from outside and immune to flex. It is heavier than most masks and built like it; this is the frameless for divers who are hard on kit. Ultra-clear, colour-true glass is standard.

items.name: Cressi F1 Frameless
items.blurb: The most affordable way into a frameless design: one wide tempered lens, a soft crystal-silicone skirt and a flat-folding profile.
items.review: The most affordable way into a frameless design: one wide tempered lens, a soft crystal-silicone skirt and a flat-folding profile. Volume is low, clearing is easy, and the fit favours narrow-to-medium faces. Buy it as a first mask; when you upgrade it becomes the pocket backup — few pieces of dive kit earn their price as quickly.

categories.title: Top Masks in 2026
categories.article_intro: A mask is the cheapest piece of kit you'll buy and the one that can ruin a dive fastest. A poor skirt seal means a dive spent clearing water; a narrow lens means missing the eagle ray that glides past your shoulder. The ten masks below are the ones working divers and instructors keep choosing, year after year. They split into two camps: frameless designs that fold flat and give a huge field of view, and twin-lens frames that fit narrow faces and take prescription lenses. Every pick here uses tempered glass and 100% silicone skirts — no PVC anywhere.
tips: Fit test: press the mask to your face without the strap and inhale through your nose — it should hold on suction alone.
Black skirts cut glare and reflections for photographers; clear skirts feel less claustrophobic for new divers.
Low-volume masks clear with a single breath and make mask-equalising on descent almost automatic.
If you wear glasses, pick a twin-lens frame model that accepts prescription drop-in lenses.

items.name: Mares Avanti Quattro+
items.blurb: Four longitudinal channels flex the blade into a scoop on every stroke, moving a large volume of water at a moderate kick rate — power that holds against current without shredding your calves.
items.review: Four longitudinal channels flex the blade into a scoop on every stroke, moving a large volume of water at a moderate kick rate — power that holds against current without shredding your calves. The long blade rewards a committed flutter kick and frog-kicks acceptably. Bungee straps come fitted, and the fin routinely survives years in rental fleets, which says everything about durability.

items.name: Scubapro Seawing Nova
items.blurb: A hinged joint behind the foot pocket lets the whole blade pivot like a propeller, so the wing sweeps water through the full stroke instead of only at the tip — quick acceleration with a cadence that is gentle on knees and ankles.
items.review: A hinged joint behind the foot pocket lets the whole blade pivot like a propeller, so the wing sweeps water through the full stroke instead of only at the tip — quick acceleration with a cadence that is gentle on knees and ankles. Cast in one piece of tough Monprene, there is nothing to crack or delaminate. For divers who want paddle speed with a softer, joint-friendly kick.

items.name: Scubapro Jet Fin
items.blurb: Short, stiff, heavy and essentially unchanged for decades because the shape is right: vented rubber blades give precise frog kicks, back kicks and helicopter turns, which is why drysuit and doubles divers still default to them.
items.review: Short, stiff, heavy and essentially unchanged for decades because the shape is right: vented rubber blades give precise frog kicks, back kicks and helicopter turns, which is why drysuit and doubles divers still default to them. The negative buoyancy balances drysuit feet. Sizing runs large — fit spring straps and the fins will outlast most of the rest of your kit.

items.name: Apeks RK3
items.blurb: A modern take on the rubber paddle: a wider, shorter blade for manoeuvring precision in confined or silty spaces, vents that smooth the recovery stroke, and oversized spring straps you can grab in 5mm gloves.
items.review: A modern take on the rubber paddle: a wider, shorter blade for manoeuvring precision in confined or silty spaces, vents that smooth the recovery stroke, and oversized spring straps you can grab in 5mm gloves. Made in several compound stiffnesses to match how heavily you dive. The shape of choice for drysuit, wreck and cold-water divers.

items.name: TUSA HyFlex Switch
items.blurb: The blade detaches from the foot pocket in seconds, so a full-size fin packs to half length in a dive bag — a travel solution no folding gimmick matches.
items.review: The blade detaches from the foot pocket in seconds, so a full-size fin packs to half length in a dive bag — a travel solution no folding gimmick matches. Assembled, it behaves like a conventional mid-stiffness paddle with a crisp snap at the end of each stroke, and the 20-degree blade angle keeps your kick in the propulsive zone.

items.name: Scubapro Go Sport
items.blurb: Built for divers who fly: about 700 grams per fin, short enough for carry-on, with a self-adjusting bungee that fits over boots or bare feet.
items.review: Built for divers who fly: about 700 grams per fin, short enough for carry-on, with a self-adjusting bungee that fits over boots or bare feet. One-piece Monprene construction takes airport handling and boat decks without a mark. Blade power is honest mid-range — ample for most reef diving, working harder in strong current.

items.name: Atomic Aquatics SplitFin
items.blurb: Water accelerating through the split generates lift like a propeller rather than push like a paddle, so a fast, shallow flutter kick becomes nearly effortless — genuine relief for knees, ankles and hips.
items.review: Water accelerating through the split generates lift like a propeller rather than push like a paddle, so a fast, shallow flutter kick becomes nearly effortless — genuine relief for knees, ankles and hips. The trade-off is real: frog and reverse kicks lose authority. For covering reef at an easy cruise, very little touches it.

items.name: Cressi Frog Plus
items.blurb: A no-nonsense paddle with side rails that channel water and a stiffness pitched for everyday reef diving.
items.review: A no-nonsense paddle with side rails that channel water and a stiffness pitched for everyday reef diving. It is among the most common fins bolted onto rental boots anywhere, because operators keep buying what keeps working. Light enough to travel, strong enough for moderate current, priced for a first set of owned kit.

items.name: Aqua Lung Storm
items.blurb: A one-piece Monprene travel fin whose raised wing-tip edges keep water on the blade even with an imperfect kick — forgiving while your technique develops.
items.review: A one-piece Monprene travel fin whose raised wing-tip edges keep water on the blade even with an imperfect kick — forgiving while your technique develops. The moulded bungee has a wide heel tab and doubles as a carry handle. Compact, quick to rinse and dry, and a natural match for warm-water diving in boots or bare feet.

items.name: Mares Power Plana LT
items.blurb: The geometry of the classic tec rubber fin cast in a lighter compound: the short-blade frog-kick precision stays, while the weight drops enough to matter at airline check-in.
items.review: The geometry of the classic tec rubber fin cast in a lighter compound: the short-blade frog-kick precision stays, while the weight drops enough to matter at airline check-in. The vented blade smooths the upstroke and stainless spring straps come fitted. For drysuit and wreck divers who want that control without the mass.

categories.title: Top Fins in 2026
categories.article_intro: Fins are where opinions get loudest — paddle versus split, short and stiff versus long and soft, rubber versus composite. Strip away the tribalism and the consensus across reviewers is remarkably stable: the same ten fins keep winning tests year after year, several of them decades-old designs that simply refuse to be beaten. Your choice comes down to diving style: strong-current reef diving rewards a stiff paddle you can frog-kick, travel diving rewards light weight and compact length, and cold-water boat diving rewards a spring-strap rubber workhorse that shrugs off a decade of abuse.
tips: Open-heel fins with boots are the default for most diving; full-foot only for warm-water boat dives.
Swap bungee or spring straps in for buckles — cheap upgrade, no more fiddling with gloved hands.
Stiffer blades transmit more power but tire untrained legs; efficiency matters more than raw stiffness.
Check the weight if you fly to dive — rubber classics can eat 4kg of your baggage allowance.

items.name: Scubapro MK25 EVO / S620 Ti
items.blurb: The MK25 EVO first stage is an air-balanced flow-through piston that delivers a huge, unwavering volume of gas regardless of tank pressure or depth, with thermal insulation that lets a piston design work in cold water.
items.review: The MK25 EVO first stage is an air-balanced flow-through piston that delivers a huge, unwavering volume of gas regardless of tank pressure or depth, with thermal insulation that lets a piston design work in cold water. The S620 Ti second stage pairs a titanium barrel with a large diaphragm for a light, dry breathe that stays composed on hard demand. Service is available in nearly every dive town on Earth.

items.name: Atomic Aquatics T3
items.blurb: Every metal part that touches gas or water is titanium, so corrosion never starts — the reason this regulator is rated for three years or 300 dives between services, twice the usual interval.
items.review: Every metal part that touches gas or water is titanium, so corrosion never starts — the reason this regulator is rated for three years or 300 dives between services, twice the usual interval. The balanced piston first stage feeds a self-adjusting second that keeps breathing effort flat from surface to depth. The lightest full-metal regulator made; the price makes sense spread over a decade of ownership.

items.name: Apeks XTX50
items.blurb: Paired with the over-balanced, environmentally sealed DST first stage, this is a reference cold-water regulator: the sealed diaphragm keeps water — and therefore freezing — out of the mechanism entirely.
items.review: Paired with the over-balanced, environmentally sealed DST first stage, this is a reference cold-water regulator: the sealed diaphragm keeps water — and therefore freezing — out of the mechanism entirely. The pneumatically balanced second stage breathes softly at any angle and converts between left- and right-hand hose routing, useful for stage bottles. A regulator you grow into, not out of.

items.name: Aqua Lung Leg3nd
items.blurb: The over-balanced diaphragm first stage raises intermediate pressure faster than ambient as you descend, so the breathe feels easier deeper, not harder.
items.review: The over-balanced diaphragm first stage raises intermediate pressure faster than ambient as you descend, so the breathe feels easier deeper, not harder. Heat-exchanger ribs around the second-stage valve keep it dependable in cold water. A does-everything set at a mainstream price, backed by one of the largest service networks in diving.

items.name: Scubapro MK19 EVO BT / G260 Carbon BT
items.blurb: A dry-sealed diaphragm first stage in black-tech finish feeding the G260 — the large-diaphragm, metal-valve second stage that technical divers standardised on for deep, cold, long dives.
items.review: A dry-sealed diaphragm first stage in black-tech finish feeding the G260 — the large-diaphragm, metal-valve second stage that technical divers standardised on for deep, cold, long dives. The carbon-fibre front cover sheds jaw-borne weight and shrugs off boat wells and surf. Reversible hose routing and generous port angles suit doubles and stage configurations.

items.name: Apeks MTX-RC
items.blurb: Developed from a military requirement for regulators that keep working below freezing: the first-stage endcap is over-moulded so ice cannot bridge its moving parts, and the all-metal second stage resists free-flow in water that stops other designs.
items.review: Developed from a military requirement for regulators that keep working below freezing: the first-stage endcap is over-moulded so ice cannot bridge its moving parts, and the all-metal second stage resists free-flow in water that stops other designs. It breathes calm and dry in strong current. Overkill for the tropics — exactly right for cold, dark and demanding diving.

items.name: Mares Epic Adj 82X
items.blurb: The twin-DFC first stage maintains two independent dynamic flow circuits, so a buddy breathing hard off your octopus doesn't lean on your own supply.
items.review: The twin-DFC first stage maintains two independent dynamic flow circuits, so a buddy breathing hard off your octopus doesn't lean on your own supply. The all-metal, adjustable second stage lets you dial cracking effort mid-dive, and its mass doubles as a heat sink for cold-water composure. Notably quiet and dry in current.

items.name: Hollis 500SE DC7
items.blurb: The 500SE is a side-exhaust second stage: bubbles vent past your ear instead of across your field of view — photographers and videographers notice on the first dive.
items.review: The 500SE is a side-exhaust second stage: bubbles vent past your ear instead of across your field of view — photographers and videographers notice on the first dive. Its servo-assisted valve delivers gas smoothly at very low cracking effort, and the environmentally sealed DC7 first stage makes the set cold-water capable. The compact body sits lightly in the jaw on long dives.

items.name: Aqua Lung Core Supreme
items.blurb: The value benchmark: environmentally sealed diaphragm first stage, heat exchanger on the second, full cold-water certification — the complete safety feature set for roughly a third of flagship money.
items.review: The value benchmark: environmentally sealed diaphragm first stage, heat exchanger on the second, full cold-water certification — the complete safety feature set for roughly a third of flagship money. An auto-closure device seals the first stage the moment it's depressurised, keeping water out during rinsing. You give up a touch of suppleness at depth and nothing that matters.

items.name: Sherwood SR2
items.blurb: A balanced-piston first stage of famous mechanical simplicity — the dry-bleed system keeps the spring chamber sealed without extra diaphragms to service — matched to a balanced second stage with a dive/pre-dive switch.
items.review: A balanced-piston first stage of famous mechanical simplicity — the dry-bleed system keeps the spring chamber sealed without extra diaphragms to service — matched to a balanced second stage with a dive/pre-dive switch. Fewer parts means fewer failure points, and cold-water dive shops have trusted the design for years. A workhorse that rewards owners who keep kit for the long haul.

categories.title: Top Regulators in 2026
categories.article_intro: A regulator is life-support, and the good news is that every model on this list breathes superbly — modern balanced regulators long ago passed the point where breathing effort separates them. What separates them now is cold-water capability, service network, travel weight and the feel of the breathe: some divers like a regulator that almost pushes air at you, others want firm, dry control in a current. Our ten picks pair the consensus first stages and seconds from Scubapro, Apeks, Atomic, Aqua Lung, Mares, Hollis and Sherwood — from no-compromise flagships to workhorses that cost a third as much and give up very little.
tips: Buying for cold water (below 10°C)? Insist on an environmentally sealed first stage — Apeks and the MK19 EVO excel here.
DIN fits everything with an insert; yoke is still the rental-tank standard in the Caribbean and Asia.
Factor servicing: a cheap regulator with no local service centre is expensive; Scubapro and Aqua Lung have the widest networks.
Titanium models resist corrosion and cut weight, but most can't be O2-cleaned for nitrox above 40%.

items.name: Scubapro Hydros Pro
items.blurb: The harness is moulded Monprene gel rather than fabric: it shapes to your torso in the water, absorbs nothing (so it dries instantly and never sours), and is close to impossible to tear.
items.review: The harness is moulded Monprene gel rather than fabric: it shapes to your torso in the water, absorbs nothing (so it dries instantly and never sours), and is close to impossible to tear. Everything is modular — weight system, pockets and rigid elements swap or strip for travel. The back-inflate bladder trims you flat and horizontal. A buy-once BCD in every respect.

items.name: Aqua Lung Outlaw
items.blurb: A rethink of what a BCD must be: about 1.7kg, a minimalist back-inflate harness that rolls to the size of a towel, sized like clothing rather than endlessly adjusted.
items.review: A rethink of what a BCD must be: about 1.7kg, a minimalist back-inflate harness that rolls to the size of a towel, sized like clothing rather than endlessly adjusted. In the water there is simply nothing around your chest — a freedom that converts many divers permanently. Modular weight options complete a rig made for travellers and warm-water divers.

items.name: Cressi Travelight
items.blurb: Full weight integration, a padded backplate that folds flat, tough 210-denier fabric and honest lift at roughly 2.6kg — your wetsuit weighs more.
items.review: Full weight integration, a padded backplate that folds flat, tough 210-denier fabric and honest lift at roughly 2.6kg — your wetsuit weighs more. The jacket-style air cell keeps surface stability familiar for newer divers, and the pockets and D-rings survive the diet. The default answer when someone wants one BCD that can fly anywhere.

items.name: Mares Dragon SLS
items.blurb: The load-distributing harness works like a trekking backpack: tank weight rides on your hips through a padded waist frame instead of hanging off your shoulders — after a week of three-dive days the difference is unmistakable.
items.review: The load-distributing harness works like a trekking backpack: tank weight rides on your hips through a padded waist frame instead of hanging off your shoulders — after a week of three-dive days the difference is unmistakable. SLS weight pockets lock with a visible green indicator, so a half-seated pocket cannot silently drop. Comfort-first engineering for high-volume divers.

items.name: Zeagle Ranger
items.blurb: Ballistic-nylon back-inflate with 20kg of lift, a rip-cord weight dump and a harness replaceable strap by strap — dived hard for thirty years because every part takes abuse and every part can be renewed.
items.review: Ballistic-nylon back-inflate with 20kg of lift, a rip-cord weight dump and a harness replaceable strap by strap — dived hard for thirty years because every part takes abuse and every part can be renewed. Equally at home over a 3mm shorty or a drysuit, and unbothered by heavy cylinders. Heavy to fly with; built to never quit.

items.name: Scubapro Level
items.blurb: The premium range's fit and finish in a lighter, simplified everyday jacket: proper zipped pockets, metal D-rings and quality webbing kept, weight pared where it doesn't cost function.
items.review: The premium range's fit and finish in a lighter, simplified everyday jacket: proper zipped pockets, metal D-rings and quality webbing kept, weight pared where it doesn't cost function. A balanced choice for divers who want one conventional, comfortable jacket for local diving and the occasional trip without paying flagship money.

items.name: Aqua Lung Pro HD
items.blurb: The workhorse of rental fleets worldwide — the strongest endorsement a BCD can earn, because operators only re-buy what survives.
items.review: The workhorse of rental fleets worldwide — the strongest endorsement a BCD can earn, because operators only re-buy what survives. Weight-integrated pockets with positive mechanical clips, a wrap-around bladder for relaxed surface floating, and dense fabric that tolerates careless boats. As a first owned BCD it does everything correctly and leaves budget for the regulator.

items.name: Apeks Exotec
items.blurb: An exoskeleton rather than a vest: a rigid spine plate with articulated shoulder joints lets the rig move with your torso as you twist and reach, so the sensation in the water is closer to wearing nothing than wearing a jacket.
items.review: An exoskeleton rather than a vest: a rigid spine plate with articulated shoulder joints lets the rig move with your torso as you twist and reach, so the sensation in the water is closer to wearing nothing than wearing a jacket. The adjustment range fits unusual builds properly. Premium price, striking engineering, and singular in-water comfort.

items.name: xDeep Zeos 28
items.blurb: A true backplate-and-wing sold as one box: stainless-steel plate, 28lb donut wing and a continuous-webbing harness with just enough padding.
items.review: A true backplate-and-wing sold as one box: stainless-steel plate, 28lb donut wing and a continuous-webbing harness with just enough padding. It trims you perfectly horizontal and strips away everything unnecessary — and it grows with you, taking a larger wing or doubles hardware on the same plate later. The natural step for divers drawn toward technical configuration.

items.name: Hollis LTS
items.blurb: The Light Travel System puts wing-style trim and full weight integration into a soft-structure rig that folds into a dive bag.
items.review: The Light Travel System puts wing-style trim and full weight integration into a soft-structure rig that folds into a dive bag. Hardware and materials come from the technical range; the price sits mid-market. For divers who want back-inflate trim and travel weight without committing to a full backplate setup yet.

categories.title: Top BCDs in 2026
categories.article_intro: The buoyancy compensator is the piece of kit you wear on every single dive, and the market has split three ways: traditional jackets that hug you reassuringly, back-inflate wings that trim you flat for photography, and minimalist travel rigs that weigh barely two kilos. The ten BCDs here are the ones instructors, divemasters and travelling divers actually wear. The Hydros Pro's near-indestructible Monprene gel harness has made it the default recommendation for divers who dive a lot, while the featherweight Outlaw and Travelight own the carry-on-only crowd, and the Ranger and Zeos serve divers heading toward doubles and technical rigs.
tips: Lift capacity should match your exposure suit: 10-15kg is plenty tropical, 20kg+ for drysuit diving.
Back-inflate trims you horizontal underwater but can push your face forward at the surface — practise before you buy.
Weight-integrated pockets must release with one obvious pull; test it in the shop.
Travelling? Anything under 2.5kg saves a wetsuit's worth of baggage weight.

items.name: Shearwater Peregrine
items.blurb: A palm-sized colour screen readable across a boat deck, running full Bühlmann ZHL-16C with adjustable gradient factors — the same transparent decompression model as technical computers, in a simple recreational package.
items.review: A palm-sized colour screen readable across a boat deck, running full Bühlmann ZHL-16C with adjustable gradient factors — the same transparent decompression model as technical computers, in a simple recreational package. It charges wirelessly, syncs logs to your phone and needs no manual. For most divers this is both the first computer and the last.

items.name: Garmin Descent Mk3i
items.blurb: A complete dive computer inside a premium multisport watch: air integration with up to ten tanks via transceivers, AMOLED screen, 200m rating, and full mapping, training and health tracking between dives.
items.review: A complete dive computer inside a premium multisport watch: air integration with up to ten tanks via transceivers, AMOLED screen, 200m rating, and full mapping, training and health tracking between dives. If you already live on the watch ecosystem it removes a whole device from your life. Battery runs weeks as a watch and full dive days in dive mode.

items.name: Shearwater Perdix 2
items.blurb: The technical benchmark: trimix, multi-gas switching, closed-circuit support and adjustable gradient factors behind a 2.2-inch screen that stays readable in silt.
items.review: The technical benchmark: trimix, multi-gas switching, closed-circuit support and adjustable gradient factors behind a 2.2-inch screen that stays readable in silt. A titanium bezel and a user-replaceable AA battery reflect its expedition intent. Recreational divers buy it because it will still be the right computer when their diving grows — nothing about it expires.

items.name: Suunto Ocean
items.blurb: A full sports watch with GPS and wrist heart-rate that becomes a serious dive computer underwater: bright AMOLED screen, Bühlmann decompression with adjustable conservatism, and air integration via a tank pod.
items.review: A full sports watch with GPS and wrist heart-rate that becomes a serious dive computer underwater: bright AMOLED screen, Bühlmann decompression with adjustable conservatism, and air integration via a tank pod. Dives and workouts sync to one app; one charger covers your whole life. The strongest all-in-one answer from a dedicated dive brand.

items.name: Garmin Descent G1
items.blurb: The rugged button-operated sibling: 45 grams, solar-assisted charging that makes cables optional on a dive trip, a high-contrast monochrome display readable in direct sun, and the full dive feature set including multi-gas.
items.review: The rugged button-operated sibling: 45 grams, solar-assisted charging that makes cables optional on a dive trip, a high-contrast monochrome display readable in direct sun, and the full dive feature set including multi-gas. It skips the colour screen and its price — and keeps everything a travelling diver actually uses.

items.name: Suunto Zoop Novo
items.blurb: Big segment digits, five buttons, a deliberately conservative decompression model that keeps new divers far from trouble, and a battery you change yourself with a coin.
items.review: Big segment digits, five buttons, a deliberately conservative decompression model that keeps new divers far from trouble, and a battery you change yourself with a coin. Nitrox to 50%, a planning mode, and nothing to misunderstand. It remains the world's most common first computer because it does less — and that is the argument.

items.name: Cressi Leonardo
items.blurb: One button, one clear screen, air and nitrox modes and a user-changeable battery: the least expensive credible dive computer from a major manufacturer, which is why it's on rental wrists on every continent.
items.review: One button, one clear screen, air and nitrox modes and a user-changeable battery: the least expensive credible dive computer from a major manufacturer, which is why it's on rental wrists on every continent. The single-button interface takes a minute to learn and never confuses afterwards. A genuine safety upgrade over renting, for the price of a few boat dives.

items.name: Mares Puck Pro+
items.blurb: The puck format grown up: large digits, an intuitive two-button flow, nitrox to 99% with gas switching, and Bluetooth upload of your logbook to the phone app.
items.review: The puck format grown up: large digits, an intuitive two-button flow, nitrox to 99% with gas switching, and Bluetooth upload of your logbook to the phone app. Slim enough to wear as a daily watch and priced under most entry rivals while doing more. The sensible middle ground between bare-bones and colour-screen money.

items.name: Aqualung i330R
items.blurb: A crisp colour screen and a USB-rechargeable battery at an entry price — legibility that used to cost three times as much.
items.review: A crisp colour screen and a USB-rechargeable battery at an entry price — legibility that used to cost three times as much. Three-gas nitrox support means it survives your advanced and deep courses instead of being outgrown, and the two-button interface stays simple throughout. A modern first computer for divers who already know they'll keep going.

items.name: Apeks DSX
items.blurb: A technical instrument through and through: full trimix and closed-circuit support, four customisable dive views, air integration via transmitters, and a vibration alert you feel through a hood when audible alarms disappear.
items.review: A technical instrument through and through: full trimix and closed-circuit support, four customisable dive views, air integration via transmitters, and a vibration alert you feel through a hood when audible alarms disappear. Every button has a machined, deliberate action. Built for divers already deep in cold-water kit and heading deeper.

categories.title: Top Dive Computers in 2026
categories.article_intro: No category of dive gear has moved faster. Ten years ago a colour screen was exotic; today the mid-market standard is a phone-bright AMOLED display, Bluetooth log sync and a rechargeable battery, and the watch-style computers double as daily fitness trackers. Two brands anchor the top of every reviewer's list: Shearwater, whose Peregrine and Perdix 2 are the recreational and tec benchmarks respectively, and Garmin, whose Descent range won over divers who want one watch for everything. Below them, the Zoop Novo, Leonardo and Puck Pro+ keep doing what they've always done — reliable, readable decompression guidance for the price of a fin set.
tips: Buy the screen you can read at 30m with fogged lenses — big numerals beat extra features.
Conservative vs liberal algorithms differ by minutes of no-deco time; Bühlmann with adjustable gradient factors is the transparent choice.
Air integration is genuinely useful but adds a transmitter (~$300) — budget for it or skip it consciously.
If it's also your watch, check battery life in dive mode; if it's dive-only, a user-changeable battery saves service costs.

items.name: Fourth Element Proteus II
items.blurb: Warmth beyond its millimetres: fleece-lined chest and back panels trap a stable layer of water over your core where heat loss is fastest, while high-stretch limbs keep the donning fight short.
items.review: Warmth beyond its millimetres: fleece-lined chest and back panels trap a stable layer of water over your core where heat loss is fastest, while high-stretch limbs keep the donning fight short. Glued and blind-stitched seams stop flush-through. The cut fits athletic and ordinary bodies alike — this is the suit divers replace with the same suit.

items.name: Scubapro Everflex Yulex Steamer
items.blurb: The first mainstream dive steamer built entirely from natural-rubber Yulex instead of petroleum neoprene, cutting the manufacturing footprint dramatically with no measurable loss of warmth or stretch.
items.review: The first mainstream dive steamer built entirely from natural-rubber Yulex instead of petroleum neoprene, cutting the manufacturing footprint dramatically with no measurable loss of warmth or stretch. Solvent-free glues and recycled linings finish the job, and the seams are glued and blind-stitched like any premium suit. The sustainable choice no longer asks for a performance sacrifice.

items.name: Bare Reactive
items.blurb: The lining is the point: ceramic-infused fibres reflect your body heat back as infrared, measurably extending comfortable time in the water over a standard-lined suit of the same thickness.
items.review: The lining is the point: ceramic-infused fibres reflect your body heat back as infrared, measurably extending comfortable time in the water over a standard-lined suit of the same thickness. Full-stretch neoprene keeps mobility high, and seams are glued and blind-stitched. The suit to choose if you're always the first person cold on the boat.

items.name: Bare Evoke (Women's)
items.blurb: The same heat-reflecting interior technology in a pattern engineered for women from the first stitch — not a shrunk men's suit: proportions through the hips, bust and torso that seal without pressure points.
items.review: The same heat-reflecting interior technology in a pattern engineered for women from the first stitch — not a shrunk men's suit: proportions through the hips, bust and torso that seal without pressure points. Warmth performance matches its sibling suit millimetre for millimetre. Consistently the women's suit that gets repurchased in the next thickness.

items.name: Mares Reef
items.blurb: A 3mm tropical workhorse: abrasion-resistant panels where ladders, tanks and reef contact chew suits, stretchier neoprene where you bend.
items.review: A 3mm tropical workhorse: abrasion-resistant panels where ladders, tanks and reef contact chew suits, stretchier neoprene where you bend. Flatlock seams keep it comfortable in warm water and the price keeps it realistic to own rather than rent. Dive operators buy them by the crate — the strongest kind of field testing there is.

items.name: Cressi Fast
items.blurb: A proper 5mm back-zip steamer priced under most 3mm suits: anatomically pre-bent arms and legs, smooth-skin wrist and ankle seals that slow flushing, and durable stitching throughout.
items.review: A proper 5mm back-zip steamer priced under most 3mm suits: anatomically pre-bent arms and legs, smooth-skin wrist and ankle seals that slow flushing, and durable stitching throughout. Donning takes patience, as with any value 5mm. For temperate-water divers who need real thermal protection without a premium outlay, this is the shortlist.

items.name: Aqua Lung AquaFlex
items.blurb: Ultra-stretch neoprene through the entire suit, not just panels: it goes on and off with an ease owners of stiffer suits don't believe until they try it, and the sizing run accommodates real-world bodies unusually well.
items.review: Ultra-stretch neoprene through the entire suit, not just panels: it goes on and off with an ease owners of stiffer suits don't believe until they try it, and the sizing run accommodates real-world bodies unusually well. Glued and blind-stitched seams; available in 3, 5 and 7mm to cover tropical through temperate water.

items.name: O'Neill Reactor-2
items.blurb: A surf-bred suit that crossed over to diving for good reasons: fluid seam welds outside the stitching keep water out, ultra-stretch panels make it comfortable all day, and the price forgives a first season's scrapes.
items.review: A surf-bred suit that crossed over to diving for good reasons: fluid seam welds outside the stitching keep water out, ultra-stretch panels make it comfortable all day, and the price forgives a first season's scrapes. The 3/2mm build is made for genuinely warm water — treat it as a tropical dive suit and it excels.

items.name: Waterproof W30
items.blurb: A 2.5mm tropical suit built with cold-water discipline: anti-slip shoulder print to hold a BCD in place, a proper zip garage, wrist and ankle seals that actually stop flushing, and patterning taken from the company's expedition suits.
items.review: A 2.5mm tropical suit built with cold-water discipline: anti-slip shoulder print to hold a BCD in place, a proper zip garage, wrist and ankle seals that actually stop flushing, and patterning taken from the company's expedition suits. The premium choice when a shorty won't do but heat isn't the enemy.

items.name: Henderson Thermoprene
items.blurb: The fleet standard of dive shops across the Americas: dependable warmth, a neoprene compound with meaningfully more stretch than standard, and a size chart from XS to 5XL with short and tall variants — nearly every diver fits one off the rack.
items.review: The fleet standard of dive shops across the Americas: dependable warmth, a neoprene compound with meaningfully more stretch than standard, and a size chart from XS to 5XL with short and tall variants — nearly every diver fits one off the rack. Panels are repairable and replaceable, which keeps these suits in service for years.

categories.title: Top Wetsuits in 2026
categories.article_intro: Wetsuit buying is thickness-first: 3mm for water above 24°C, 5mm for 18-24°C, 7mm or semi-dry below that. But within a thickness class the differences are real — panel stretch, seam construction, zipper quality and the linings that decide whether a suit feels warm the moment you pull it on. This list leans on long-term owner experience across thousands of dives. Fourth Element's Proteus II keeps its crown for warmth-to-flexibility, Bare's Reactive and Evoke bring the infrared-reflecting linings from their drysuit line, and Scubapro's Everflex Yulex leads the industry's move away from petroleum neoprene entirely.
tips: Fit beats thickness: a snug 3mm outperforms a baggy 5mm because trapped water stays put.
Glued-and-blindstitched seams keep water out; flatlock seams are for warm water only.
Back-zips are easy solo; front-zips flex more and seal better but need practice.
Rinse in fresh water and dry inside-out in shade — sun-baked neoprene loses its stretch in a season.
